text: Welcome to your Frontend Engineer mock interview. Today we will focus on React performance and Next.js.
Thank you! I am excited to be here. I have been working with React and Next.js for about three years now.
Great. Let's start with a core concept: can you explain how React's virtual DOM works and how reconciliation optimizes rendering?

Sure. The virtual DOM is a lightweight JavaScript representation of the real DOM. When state changes, React creates a new virtual DOM tree and compares it with the previous one using a diffing algorithm called reconciliation. Instead of updating the entire real DOM, React only updates the specific elements that actually changed, which is much faster and reduces layout reflows.
That's an excellent explanation. You mentioned the diffing algorithm. What is the complexity of React's diffing algorithm and how does React optimize it to run in O(n) time?

In the general case, comparing two trees has a complexity of O(n^3). React optimizes this to O(n) by using two main heuristics. First, two elements of different types will produce different trees, so React tears down the old tree and builds the new one from scratch. Second, developers can provide a 'key' prop to hint which elements remain stable across renders, allowing React to match children across updates efficiently.
Spot on. Let's move to Next.js. What is the difference between Server Components and Client Components in App Router, and when would you use each?

React Server Components, or RSC, render exclusively on the server. They help reduce client-side bundle size because their dependencies are not shipped to the browser, and they allow direct database querying. Client components are marked with the 'use client' directive and run in the browser. You use Client Components when you need user interactivity, like onClick handlers, React hooks like useState or useEffect, or browser-exclusive APIs. You use Server Components by default for static content, SEO optimization, and data fetching.
Perfect. You have a very strong grasp of both React rendering mechanics and modern Next.js architecture. I think you did a fantastic job today. Do you have any questions for me?
Thank you! Yes, I'd love to know what the engineering team's biggest challenge is right now with Next.js migration.
